food_subgroup: Vermicelli noodle platter with grilled meats and fried spring roll

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Bánh Hỏi with mixed BBQ meats and spring roll. FOODON_03311069 (rice vermicelli dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Rice noodles) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Bánh Hỏi composite platter with grilled meats and spring roll.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Vietnamese vermicelli platter dish.

preparation_method: grilled (pork, chicken, nem nuong, beef in betel leaves)
main_ingredients: beef in betel leaves (bò lá lốt)

serving.natural_language_portion: One full platter of Bánh Hỏi Đặc Biệt typically weighs approximately 450 g and includes a portion of woven rice vermicelli, assorted grilled meats (pork, chicken, nem nuong, beef in betel leaves), and one or two crispy spring rolls, served with fresh herbs and dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: rice vermicelli, grilled pork, grilled chicken, fried spring roll components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo or USDA entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 40% rice vermicelli (HPB Foodo: bee hoon, cooked), 35% mixed grilled meats (HPB Foodo: char siew, grilled chicken; USDA SR Legacy: ground pork patty grilled, chicken breast grilled), 15% crispy spring roll (HPB Foodo: spring roll fried), 10% herbs and dipping sauce. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this platter type. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice vermicelli / rice noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bánh Hỏi Đặc Biệt composite platter. GI of 53 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: rice vermicelli / rice noodles (GI ~53, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 International GI Tables and the University of Sydney GI Database. The mixed grilled protein and fried components lower the effective GI of the meal. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 89.1) / 100 ≈ 47.2; however, using the composite per-100g carbohydrate of 19.8 g × 450 g serving / 100 = 89.1 g carbohydrate per serving, GL = 47.2. Reported GL per serving is 47.2 rounded; note this is a large platter. For a standard 200 g portion GL ≈ 21.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1890 mg per full 450 g platter) from dipping sauce, marinades, and processed spring roll filling
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced dipping sauce, as a full platter can deliver approximately 1890 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~53) from rice vermicelli base, partially offset by protein and fat from grilled meats; large carbohydrate load per full platter (~89 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a half-portion (approximately 200–225 g) to limit carbohydrate intake to approximately 40–45 g per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response given the high total carbohydrate load of the full platter.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.3 g per 100 g) from grilled pork and fried spring roll, combined with high sodium from dipping sauce and marinades
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to a half-portion, avoid or minimise the dipping sauce to reduce sodium intake, and prefer the grilled meat components over the deep-fried spring roll to lower saturated fat and trans fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Hỏi Đặc Biệt, served in Singapore as 'Super Thin Vermicelli with Mixed BBQ and Crispy Spring Roll', is a Vietnamese specialty noodle platter centred on bánh hỏi — extremely fine, woven rice vermicelli sheets that are steamed and arranged in delicate layered bundles. The platter is served with an assortment of grilled meats including char-grilled pork (thịt nướng), grilled chicken, nem nuong (Vietnamese grilled pork sausage patties), and bò lá lốt (minced beef wrapped and grilled in betel leaves), alongside one or more crispy deep-fried spring rolls (chả giò). The dish is typically accompanied by fresh herbs such as mint, perilla, and bean sprouts, and a tangy-sweet Vietnamese dipping sauce (nước chấm) made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The texture contrast between the silky, delicate vermicelli and the smoky, charred meats and crunchy spring rolls is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, protein-rich meal with a significant carbohydrate load from the rice vermicelli base and a notable sodium contribution from the dipping sauce and marinades. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 53 (low category) based on the rice vermicelli component, but the large carbohydrate portion per full platter (~89 g) results in a high glycemic load. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertensive individuals, while the carbohydrate load requires portion management for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to its multiple meat components and fish-sauce-based condiment.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using HPB Singapore Foodo (bee hoon cooked, char siew, grilled chicken, fried spring roll) and USDA SR Legacy (grilled pork, grilled chicken breast) as component anchors. GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli dominant carbohydrate component using the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Composite dish estimate: nutrient values derived from weighted component analysis (40% rice vermicelli, 35% mixed grilled meats, 15% crispy spring roll, 10% herbs and dipping sauce) using HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for these food types. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo entries used for this estimation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo entries used for this estimation. All other -1 fields represent nutrients not reported in the primary component sources. No core macro is zero; all four core macros are positive and nutritionally plausible for this mixed meat-and-noodle platter. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated at 47.2 for the full 450 g platter; for a standard 200 g portion GL ≈ 21. The dish is served at Co Chung Restaurant, Lorong Telok, Singapore. Price SGD 36.78. Halal status unconfirmed — the restaurant name and dish composition suggest Vietnamese-style pork is present, making halal certification unlikely. Allergen list is indicative; actual allergen content depends on restaurant preparation.
